game_name: Age of Empires II: The Age of Kings

design_philosophy.core_loop: Gather resources (food, wood, gold, stone) → Build structures and train units → Advance through four historical ages (Dark, Feudal, Castle, Imperial) → Defeat opponent civilizations through military conquest or economic dominance
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity and time pressure: players must balance immediate military threats with long-term age advancement and infrastructure development; trading systems introduce risk (trade units vulnerable to enemy attack) versus reward (distance-based gold generation)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a historical civilization from medieval times through the Renaissance, making strategic decisions about economic development, military composition, and technological progression to conquer rivals
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Age advancement is gated by specific building prerequisites and resource costs, forcing commitment to progression paths rather than allowing skipping ages; 13 distinct civilizations with unique units/structures create faction identity; resource gathering requires active villager management via deposit buildings, creating logistical gameplay

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player selects opponent AI difficulty and civilization matchups; campaign scenarios impose 'specialized and story-backed conditions' that constrain player options and create mission-specific challenges

progression.upgrade_system: Age advancement unlocks new units, structures, and technologies; each civilization can research upgrades affecting resource gathering rates and unit capabilities (e.g., better armor for infantry); 13 distinct civilizations offer asymmetric progression paths
progression.unlock_mechanism: Progression to next age requires: (1) constructing specific buildings from current age, (2) paying resource cost; advancing ages gates access to new unit types and research options
replayability_hooks: 13 playable civilizations with unique units and structures create different strategic approaches
5 historically-based campaigns with specialized scenario conditions (Genghis Khan, Barbarossa, Saladin, Joan of Arc, William Wallace)
Multiplayer mode supports competitive faction play
3 additional single-player game modes beyond campaigns
Dynamic market prices and trade route optimization provide economic strategy variation

world_building_techniques: 5 historically-based campaigns contextualize civilizations within real medieval/Renaissance events
Player-controlled hero units in Joan of Arc and William Wallace campaigns personify leadership
Guiding spirits representative of army commanders provide narrative framing in other campaigns
Medieval setting (1,000-year timeframe across four ages) establishes historical world context
Civilization diversity (13 cultures) implies geopolitical complexity

faction_indicators: 13 playable civilizations with distinct units, buildings, and technologies create faction identity
Civilization choice signals player preference for economic (market trade), military (unique unit types), or technological (research speed) playstyles
danger_signals: Trade routes vulnerable to enemy unit interception
Resource depletion risk (finite stone/gold mines, tree deforestation) creates economic pressure
Age advancement requires resource commitment, creating windows of military vulnerability
environmental_storytelling: Resource nodes (gold mines, stone quarries, berry patches, livestock) establish economic geography
Monastery relics function as territorial control objectives
Market and dock placement determines trade route geometry and economic reach
Town center positioning indicates civilization settlement and defensive strategy
